Name: sentinel_augmentation_forgetting
Title: Sentinel Augmentations: Can Two Fixed Views Reveal When Supervision Should Be Trusted?
Short Hypothesis: A training example whose label is correct should eventually become correct across a small set of fixed augmented 'sentinel' views, while mislabeled or augmentation-sensitive examples will exhibit persistent view disagreement or repeated forgetting. Tracking correctness on only two deterministic sentinel augmentations per image provides a cheap signal for when to trust cross-entropy supervision versus when to replace it with consistency regularization.
Related Work: Example forgetting events were studied by Toneva et al. as a way to identify atypical or unnecessary examples. Dataset cartography and related methods analyze training dynamics to characterize easy, hard, and ambiguous examples. Label-noise methods such as MentorNet, co-teaching, DivideMix, and small-loss selection reduce the effect of suspected noisy examples, often using loss statistics, extra networks, mixture modeling, or semi-supervised pipelines. Consistency methods such as Π-model, Mean Teacher, MixMatch, FixMatch, and AugMix encourage prediction invariance across augmentations. This proposal differs by asking a simpler question: whether disagreement and forgetting across two fixed augmented views of the same training item is enough to decide how much to trust its label. The method does not require extra models, expensive augmentations, large datasets, or complex semi-supervised machinery, and it focuses on a reproducible diagnostic and intervention for small-scale settings.
Abstract: Deep networks are often trained with stochastic data augmentation, but we rarely ask whether a model learns an example itself or merely learns some augmented views of it. We propose Sentinel Augmentation Forgetting, a simple diagnostic and training rule based on two fixed augmentations per training example. Before training, each image is assigned two deterministic sentinel views sampled from the same augmentation family used during training. At the end of each epoch, the model is evaluated on these views. We record whether each sentinel is classified correctly, whether the two views agree, and whether either view transitions from correct to incorrect. Our hypothesis is that persistent sentinel disagreement is a cheap marker of unreliable supervision: it should be common for mislabeled, ambiguous, or augmentation-brittle examples, but rare for clean learnable examples after early training. We then test a minimal intervention: examples with stable sentinel correctness receive ordinary cross-entropy, while examples with repeated sentinel forgetting or disagreement have their supervised loss downweighted and are trained primarily with consistency between stochastic augmentations. This produces a lightweight alternative to loss-based label-noise filtering. The project is designed for small academic-scale experiments on CIFAR-10/100, MNIST variants, and synthetic label noise. It aims to provide both an interpretable measurement tool and a practical recipe for improving robustness without additional networks or large compute.
Experiments: Diagnostic study on clean CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, Fashion-MNIST, and corrupted MNIST variants. Train small ResNet-18 or CNN models with standard augmentation. For each training example, store two fixed sentinel augmentations. At each epoch, record sentinel correctness, sentinel agreement, and forgetting events. Measure whether persistent sentinel disagreement predicts high final loss, low confidence, misclassification, or human/plausible ambiguity.

Synthetic label-noise detection. Inject symmetric and class-dependent label noise at 20%, 40%, and 60% on CIFAR-10 and Fashion-MNIST. Compare noisy-label detection using average loss, early-loss ranking, ordinary example forgetting, and sentinel disagreement/forgetting. Metrics: AUROC, AUPRC, precision at top-k suspected noisy examples, and calibration of noise probability.

Training intervention. Implement Sentinel Loss Routing: maintain a per-example score equal to an exponential moving average of sentinel disagreement plus sentinel forgetting. If the score exceeds a threshold after a warmup period, reduce cross-entropy weight for that example and add a consistency loss between two stochastic augmentations using the model's detached prediction average as a soft target. Compare to ERM, label smoothing, mixup, co-teaching-style small-loss selection, and a simple consistency baseline. Metrics: clean test accuracy, noisy-label robustness, expected calibration error, and training stability.

Ablations for simplicity. Vary the number of sentinel views from 1 to 4, compare fixed versus resampled sentinels, compare weak versus strong sentinel augmentations, and test whether the method still works when sentinel evaluation is done every 5 epochs instead of every epoch. Report wall-clock overhead and memory overhead.

Distribution-shift robustness. Train on CIFAR-10 and evaluate on CIFAR-10-C or simple corruptions generated locally. Test whether downweighting persistently sentinel-unstable examples improves corruption accuracy and whether sentinel instability identifies examples that are augmentation artifacts rather than semantically useful hard examples.

Qualitative audit. Visualize the top 100 most sentinel-unstable examples in clean and noisy datasets. Categorize them as mislabeled, ambiguous, fine-grained, augmentation-broken, or genuinely hard. This tests whether the statistic is interpretable rather than merely correlated with loss.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Sentinel disagreement may correlate strongly with ordinary loss, making the method less novel empirically. The proposal addresses this by directly comparing against loss and standard forgetting baselines.

Some genuinely useful hard examples may be downweighted, potentially hurting accuracy on rare or fine-grained cases. Ablations should measure per-class effects and compare aggressive versus conservative thresholds.
The choice of sentinel augmentation strength matters. Too weak may be uninformative; too strong may create artificial instability. This will be handled through a small augmentation-strength sweep.
For very small datasets, fixed sentinel views might overfit or become too familiar. Periodic resampling and held-out sentinel variants can be tested as controls.
The method is primarily targeted at supervised image classification. Extension to text or large-scale settings is possible but not required for the core workshop contribution.
